title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita

GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between government expenditure per primary school student as a share of GDP per capita and the GDP per capita itself for various countries in 2008. The vertical axis represents the share of GDP per capita spent, ranging from 0% to over 40%, while the horizontal axis shows GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale from approximately $2,000 to $100,000. Countries are color-coded by region: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). Serbia stands out with the highest share, exceeding 40%, while Kazakhstan has one of the lowest shares, near 0%. Several African nations, including Burundi, Niger, and Rwanda, cluster in the lower-left quadrant, indicating both low GDP per capita (below $5,000) and relatively low spending shares (mostly under 15%). In contrast, European countries like Austria, Malta, and Cyprus appear in the upper-right area, showing higher GDP per capita (above $20,000) and spending shares between 20% and 30%. Some Asian countries, such as Thailand and South Korea, also exhibit moderate to high spending shares (around 20-30%) despite varying GDP levels. The distribution reveals no strict linear pattern; instead, it highlights diverse national priorities in education funding relative to economic output.
